worth_it_explanation: Yahoo DSP's identity architecture centers on Yahoo ConnectID, a deterministic signal built on hashed, authenticated emails from Yahoo's own logged-in user base, and extends through a partnership with LiveRamp that lets advertisers activate RampID audiences alongside ConnectID inventory. Accounts that skip this configuration and leave cookie-based audience logic in place lose addressable reach on CTV and mobile app inventory where third-party cookies do not exist. The checks here take less than an hour to complete and the corrections, mainly enabling ConnectID at the line level and confirming audience composition for cookieless environments, carry over to every future campaign on the account.
intro: Yahoo DSP uses Yahoo ConnectID as its primary deterministic identity signal. ConnectID is built on hashed emails and authenticated signals from Yahoo's owned and operated properties, including Yahoo Mail, Yahoo News, Yahoo Sports, and Yahoo Finance, covering more than 200 million authenticated users in the United States. Through a partnership with LiveRamp, Yahoo DSP also supports RampID, allowing advertisers to reach audiences across publishers who have adopted LiveRamp's Authenticated Traffic Solution (ATS). These signals matter most in environments where third-party cookies are absent: connected TV (CTV), mobile app, and authenticated web. Without an identity signal bridging those environments to desktop, frequency capping applies per device rather than per person, attribution breaks across screens, and audiences built on cookie logic match almost no users on cookieless inventory. The checks here confirm whether the account is configured to use the identity signals Yahoo DSP provides, whether cookieless inventory is targeted with audiences that can actually match on it, and whether cross-environment frequency is managed at the person level.

audit_checks.title: No alternative identifier is configured, so cookieless inventory is unaddressable
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Third-party cookies are blocked in Safari and Firefox by default and absent entirely in CTV and mobile app environments. An account that has not enabled ConnectID or a supported partner identifier such as RampID has no addressable signal on that inventory. Reach on cookieless supply is measured by raw impressions with no audience match, frequency capping does not work, and attribution cannot tie conversions back to ad exposures in those environments.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP offers two primary identity paths for addressable advertising in cookieless environments. The first is Yahoo ConnectID, a deterministic identifier built from hashed, consented emails tied to Yahoo's logged-in user base across properties such as Yahoo Mail, Yahoo Sports, and Yahoo Finance. The second path is RampID, available through Yahoo DSP's partnership with LiveRamp, which lets advertisers reach authenticated users across publishers that have deployed LiveRamp's Authenticated Traffic Solution (ATS).

Third-party cookies have been blocked in Safari and Firefox for several years and do not exist at all in connected TV (CTV) or mobile app environments. An account that has not configured either ConnectID or RampID is relying on a signal that reaches only a portion of the open web and none of the CTV or app inventory it may be buying.

Yahoo DSP's ConnectID is enabled at the line level. A line that does not have ConnectID active will serve to cookieless supply with no identity match, which means the platform cannot apply audience targeting, cannot cap frequency per person across environments, and cannot attribute a conversion that happens in a different session or on a different device from the ad exposure.

A few signs point to an account that has not configured an alternative identifier:
  • Lines running on connected TV or mobile app placements show high impression volume but low or zero audience match rates in reporting
  • Frequency settings are configured per line but delivery reports show the same users receiving far more than the stated cap on devices where cookies are absent
  • The account has not been updated since Yahoo's ConnectID partnership with LiveRamp expanded in 2024, and no line settings reflect that change
You may want to confirm whether ConnectID is enabled on lines that run CTV or mobile app inventory before those lines accumulate spend at scale.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running CTV or mobile app lines alongside display, you may want to confirm that ConnectID is active on those lines. Without it, a shopper who sees a CTV ad and later visits the site on desktop appears as a new, untracked session, and the CTV line receives no attribution credit for the conversion.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation campaign where audience quality matters more than raw scale, you may want to confirm that lines targeting mobile app inventory have ConnectID enabled. Impressions served without an identity match cannot be tied to a specific lead form submission, so cost-per-lead calculations for those lines will be understated.

learn_more.source_title: Yahoo DSP buying guide: campaign and line setup

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP's ConnectID configuration settings are managed within the Yahoo DSP platform UI. Detailed setup documentation for ConnectID at the line level requires a Yahoo DSP account login. Contact your Yahoo DSP account representative for step-by-step configuration guidance.

impactful_updates.update_title: Yahoo DSP and LiveRamp announce full integration of ConnectID with LiveRamp's ATS
impactful_updates.update_summary: Yahoo DSP and LiveRamp completed a full integration allowing advertisers in Yahoo DSP to activate RampID audiences alongside Yahoo ConnectID inventory. Publishers using LiveRamp's Authenticated Traffic Solution can now pass ConnectID as an identifier, giving Yahoo DSP advertisers broader authenticated reach across the open web, mobile, and CTV.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether ConnectID or a supported partner identifier is enabled on the account's active lines, particularly those delivering CTV or mobile app inventory.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Navigate to Advertisers and select the advertiser you are auditing.
Open an active campaign and go to its Lines list.
Select a line that runs connected TV or mobile app inventory. Open its settings and locate the identity or audience section.
Check whether ConnectID is enabled for the line. Note the current setting.
Repeat for each line that targets CTV or mobile app placements. Record which lines have ConnectID active and which do not.
If any line is missing ConnectID, note the line name, the channel type, and the impression volume for that line during the review period.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per line, whether ConnectID is enabled and whether that line delivers CTV or mobile app inventory. A line serving cookieless environments with ConnectID active passes. A line serving CTV or mobile app inventory without ConnectID enabled, or without any alternative identifier configured, is a fail recorded with the line name, channel type, and impression volume. Lines serving only cookie-supported desktop or browser environments where third-party cookies are still available are noted separately.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the ConnectID status and channel type for each active line above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more lines in {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP account are delivering to connected TV or mobile app inventory without Yahoo ConnectID or a supported partner identifier enabled. Third-party cookies do not exist in these environments, so the lines are serving without an audience identity match. Frequency caps are not enforcing at the person level, audience targeting is not applied, and conversions in those environments cannot be attributed back to the ad exposures. {{voice_recommend}} enabling ConnectID on each line that serves CTV or mobile app inventory and confirming with the Yahoo DSP account team whether RampID should also be activated for the account given its audience composition.

audit_checks.title: CTV and mobile app lines use cookie-based audience segments that do not match in cookieless environments
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Audience segments built on third-party cookie data cannot resolve against connected TV or mobile app inventory where cookies do not exist. When those segments are applied to CTV or app lines, the platform serves to unmatched inventory at scale, which means the audience definition the advertiser paid to build and apply has no effect on who sees the ad in those environments.

educate.base:
Audience segments in Yahoo DSP can be built from several sources: third-party cookie-based data, first-party advertiser data matched via ConnectID or RampID, and Yahoo's own audience data derived from its logged-in user base. These sources are not equivalent in where they can resolve.

A third-party cookie-based segment, such as a retargeting pool built from a pixel on a website or a purchased segment from a data provider that tracks users via cookies, relies on a cookie to match the segment to an eligible impression. On connected TV (CTV) inventory, no cookie exists. The TV set does not have a browser cookie, and the CTV ad server cannot look up a user in a cookie-based segment. The result is that the segment applies to the line but matches almost no users on CTV placements. The line continues to spend against the nominal audience, but the actual users reached are whoever the platform can serve to without a match, which may bear little resemblance to the defined audience.

Mobile app inventory has the same characteristic. Apps do not pass third-party cookies. An audience segment that depends on a cookie identifier is effectively inactive on mobile app supply, even when the line is configured to target that app environment.

ConnectID resolves this by providing a deterministic signal that exists in both environments. A first-party customer list matched to ConnectID, or a Yahoo audience segment derived from authenticated Yahoo user behavior, can match against CTV and app supply where ConnectID is present in the bid request. RampID operates similarly for publishers that have deployed LiveRamp's Authenticated Traffic Solution (ATS).

A few signs point to a mismatch between audience type and inventory environment:
  • A CTV or mobile app line uses a retargeting segment that was built from a website pixel, which produces cookie-based member IDs
  • Reporting for the CTV or app line shows high delivery but audience match rates are low or not reported
  • The advertiser's first-party data has not been matched to ConnectID or RampID, so the only audience option for CTV lines is a cookie-based third-party segment
You may want to review the audience source attached to each CTV and mobile app line before the campaign runs at scale.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a retargeting campaign across display and CTV, you may want to confirm that the retargeting audience used on CTV lines is matched to ConnectID rather than relying on pixel-based cookie IDs. A cookie-based retargeting pool will not match your site visitors on their televisions, so the CTV line reaches a different and unintended audience.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation campaign using a purchased intent audience segment on mobile app placements, you may want to confirm that the segment is tied to a mobile-compatible identifier rather than a third-party cookie. Intent segments built from cookie-based web behavior will not resolve against in-app inventory.

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP's audience segment compatibility with cookieless environments depends on how each segment was built and whether it is keyed to ConnectID or RampID. The Yahoo DSP platform UI and your account representative can confirm the identity signal backing each audience segment in the account.

investigate.intro: Identify the audience segments applied to CTV and mobile app lines and confirm whether those segments are keyed to an identifier that resolves in cookieless environments.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Navigate to Advertisers, select the advertiser, and open an active campaign.
Select a line configured for connected TV or mobile app inventory. Open its targeting or audience settings.

Review the audience segments applied to the line. For each segment, note the source: is it a first-party data segment matched to ConnectID or RampID, a Yahoo audience segment derived from authenticated users, or a third-party data segment?
For any third-party segment, confirm how the segment was built. If it was built from a web pixel or cookie-based data provider, note it as potentially incompatible with the CTV or app environment.
Repeat for each CTV and mobile app line in the campaign. Record the line name, channel type, segment name, and segment source.
If the advertiser has a first-party customer list, confirm whether it has been matched to ConnectID or RampID within the Yahoo DSP platform.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per CTV or mobile app line, the audience segments applied and the identity signal backing each segment. A CTV or mobile app line using a first-party segment matched to ConnectID or RampID, or a Yahoo-derived authenticated audience, passes. A CTV or mobile app line using a cookie-based third-party segment or a pixel-based retargeting audience with no ConnectID or RampID match is a fail recorded with the line name, channel type, segment name, and the segment's likely identity signal.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the audience segment and its identity signal for each CTV and mobile app line above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more CTV or mobile app lines in {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP account are using audience segments backed by cookie-based identifiers. Those segments cannot resolve against connected TV or mobile app inventory where cookies do not exist, so the lines are serving at scale without the audience match the campaign was designed around. {{voice_recommend}} replacing the cookie-based segments on CTV and mobile app lines with first-party data matched to Yahoo ConnectID or RampID, or with Yahoo audience segments derived from authenticated user behavior, so that the audience definition can actually resolve in the environments the lines are buying.

audit_checks.title: Frequency capping fragments across CTV, mobile, and desktop because no cross-environment identity signal is active
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without an identity signal that bridges CTV, mobile, and desktop into a single person-level profile, frequency caps apply per device rather than per person. The same individual can receive the campaign's stated impression cap on each device separately, multiplying actual exposure. Reported reach counts devices rather than people, overstating the number of unique individuals the campaign covered.

educate.base:
Frequency capping in Yahoo DSP limits how many times an individual sees the campaign within a defined window. The word 'individual' is the critical one: frequency capping can only enforce at the person level if the platform has a signal that recognizes the same person across the devices and environments where the campaign is running.

Yahoo ConnectID provides that cross-environment signal. It ties a person's authenticated Yahoo identity to their behavior across connected TV (CTV), mobile app, and desktop or mobile web environments. When ConnectID is active and present in the bid stream for a given impression, Yahoo DSP can recognize that the impression opportunity belongs to someone who has already seen the campaign on their phone or television, and apply the frequency cap accordingly.

Without ConnectID or a compatible partner identifier, Yahoo DSP has no mechanism to join the CTV impression to the mobile or desktop impression from the same person. A frequency cap of three impressions per person becomes a cap of three per device per environment. A person who owns a smart TV, a smartphone, and a laptop can receive three impressions on each, totaling nine, before any device trips the cap. Reported reach reflects the number of devices reached, not the number of people, which inflates the unique reach figure.

This also affects attribution. Without a cross-environment identity signal, a conversion that happens on desktop after a CTV ad exposure appears as an unrelated event. The CTV line receives no credit, and the advertiser cannot measure the full path that led to the conversion.

A few signs point to frequency fragmentation across environments:
  • The campaign includes CTV lines alongside display or mobile app lines, but ConnectID is not confirmed active on the CTV lines
  • Frequency distribution in reporting shows most users at or near the per-device cap, with overall reach higher than the budget would suggest if only unique people were being reached
  • The campaign objective is brand awareness or reach, where overspending against the same person on multiple devices is directly at odds with the efficiency goal
You may want to confirm ConnectID is active across all line types before a cross-channel campaign launches.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a cross-channel campaign that includes CTV and display, you may want to confirm that ConnectID bridges those environments so that a shopper who sees the ad on television is not also served the full display frequency allotment as a separate unrecognized person. Overexposure on the same person across devices increases creative fatigue without increasing reach.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation campaign where prospect fatigue matters, you may want to confirm person-level frequency control across CTV and digital lines. A prospect who sees the ad repeatedly across their television and phone may disengage before completing the form, and without cross-environment capping that overexposure goes undetected in per-device reporting.

learn_more.source_title: Yahoo DSP buying guide: campaign and line setup

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP's cross-environment frequency controls and ConnectID configuration are managed within the Yahoo DSP platform UI. Detailed documentation on person-level frequency settings requires a Yahoo DSP account login. Contact your Yahoo DSP account representative to confirm how ConnectID affects frequency cap enforcement across line types.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether ConnectID is active across the campaign's CTV, mobile app, and display or desktop lines, and whether the frequency cap unit matches the identity resolution level in use.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Navigate to Advertisers, select the advertiser, and open the campaign you are auditing.
On the Lines list, note which lines target connected TV, which target mobile app, and which target display or desktop web.
For each line, open its settings and confirm whether ConnectID is enabled. Record the ConnectID status per line.

Locate the frequency cap setting for each line. Note the cap value, the time window (daily, weekly, lifetime), and the unit the cap applies to (device or person, if the platform exposes that distinction).

If any line lacks ConnectID and runs CTV or mobile app inventory, note the line name, channel type, impression volume, and stated frequency cap. The cap is not enforcing at the person level across environments on that line.

Check the campaign's reach reporting if available. Compare unique users reported against the campaign budget and cost per thousand impressions (CPM). If reach appears high relative to what the budget could cover at a standard CPM, device-level counting rather than person-level counting may be inflating the figure.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per line, the channel type, ConnectID status, and the frequency cap unit. A campaign where all cross-environment lines have ConnectID active and the frequency cap aligns with person-level resolution passes. A campaign where CTV or mobile app lines lack ConnectID, or where the frequency cap is set per device while the campaign objective requires person-level control, is a fail recorded with the specific line names, channel types, and the mismatch between cap unit and identity resolution level.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the ConnectID status, channel type, and frequency cap setting for each line above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP campaign includes connected TV and digital lines without a cross-environment identity signal bridging them. Without Yahoo ConnectID active across all line types, frequency caps apply per device rather than per person, so the same individual may receive the campaign's full frequency allotment separately on their television, phone, and desktop. Reported reach counts devices rather than people, which overstates the number of unique individuals the campaign covered. {{voice_recommend}} confirming ConnectID is active on every line type in the campaign and verifying that the frequency cap setting aligns with person-level identity resolution across connected TV, mobile, and desktop environments.
